text: The following is a case report of a robotic-assisted approach to colocalonic intussusception. Patient is a thirty-six-year-old male with a known cecolipoma currently scheduled for elective resection who presented to the emergency department complaining of worsening lower abdominal pain as well as difficulty passing bowel movements. He denied any blood in his stool, nausea, vomiting, or other obstructive symptoms. Other than his known cecolipoma, he denied any past medical or past surgical history. On exam, he was afebrile with normal vital signs. His abdomen was mildly distended and tender in the right lower quadrant. His labs were within normal limits, including a white count of eight point eight.

The surgeon uses a varus needle to access the abdomen in the left upper quadrant, followed by the insertion of robotic ports. The robot is docked, and a small grasping retractor and cartier are used with the left hand, while a vessel sealer is used with the right hand. The gastrocolic ligaments at the transverse colon are divided and mobilized, the hepatic flexure is taken down, and the white line of TOLT is divided, fully mobilizing the right colon down to the cecum and terminal ileum.

The surgeon identifies and dissects the base of the ileocolic, confirming its location with indocyanine green (ICG). A robotic stapler with a white staple cartridge is used to divide the ileocolic at its base. The mesentery is divided, mobilizing ten centimeters of the terminal ileum, and the vessel sealer is used to completely divide the mesentery from the transverse colon to the terminal ileum.

Three milliliters of ICG is administered through the patient's peripheral IV, highlighting a vivid green area of terminal ileum and transverse colon. Blue staple cartridges on the robotic stapler are used to transect these sections, freeing the entire specimen. An intracorporeal isoparastaltic ileocolic anastomosis is performed, with two vipral stase deuters aligning the ileum along the transverse colon. Enterotomy and colotomy are made, and two 45mm blue loads create the anastomosis. A 3-0 V-lock is used to close the common enterocolotomy in a running fashion in two layers. The robot is undocked, and the specimen is removed through a small incision at the suprapubic port.

Final pathology was notable for a lipoma of the ileocecal valve measuring three point eight by three point five by two point two centimeters with acute inflammation and fat necrosis as well as overlying mucosa with focal inflammation, ulceration and ischemic changes. In conclusion, lipomas as the lead point in adult intussusception is rare. Robotic resection and anastomosis is a viable option that can lead to improved outcomes, shorter length of stay, and quicker recovery for the patient.
